data.title: Realty Income extends rally for seventh straight session
data.description:
Realty Income (O) shares rose 0.24% to close at $61.77 on Wednesday, extending gains for a seventh consecutive session..

The stock had gained about 4.64% between January 12 and January 20, far outpacing the S&P 500 that fell -2.59% over the same period.

U.S. real estate stocks saw mixed signals, after earlier gains driven by falling mortgage rates and a sharp rise in loan applications were offset by fresh policy and macro concerns. Commentary from President Donald Trump on housing policy, including efforts to address institutional home purchases and bolster affordability, added to positive sentiment for the industry.

That policy uncertainty came alongside weaker housing data, after pending home sales plunged far more than expected, adding to doubts about the near-term health of the housing market. Broader market risk-off pressure, with U.S. stock futures and major indices under strain, also weighed on real estate names, helping explain why Realty Income slipped even as parts of the sector had earlier shown gains.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, O is rated with a Buy, with grades of A in growth and profitability, a B in momentum, while a C in revisions and a C+ in terms of valuation.

SA analysts suggest buying the stock.

An analyst said Realty Income remains attractive for income-focused investors, citing its resilient cash flows and valuation. The analyst said the REIT “offers bond-like stability, high occupancy, and resilient AFFO growth,” adding that the stock is trading “at a discount” to its historical valuation, with forward AFFO growth projected at about 3% annually.

However, turning to the Wall Street, analysts are cautious, with seven of 25 analysts rating the stock with a Buy or higher, 17 recommending a hold, while one suggests a sell.

The stock remains up 9.14% year-to-date and has surged 9.32% higher over the past month.

data.title: Advanced Micro Devices extends winning streak to seven sessions
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ: AMD) extended its winning streak to a seventh straight session  with the stock closing at 7.69% at $249.76 on Wednesday.

AMD has added 14.15% over the course of the last six trading sessions compared to a drop of 2.43% in the benchmark S&P 500 Index during the same period. While in 2025, the stock posted a substantial growth of 11.97% compared to a drop of 0.71% in the S&P 500 Index.

Recently, AMD announced a partnership with TCS to co-develop AI and GenAI solutions for specific industries, combining TCS’s expertise and global network with AMD’s high-performance computing and AI products.

According to Seeking Alpha analyst Danil Sereda, the upcoming Venice EPYC built on TSMC’s 2 nm process, along with continued CPU market share gains, is strengthening AMD’s high-margin growth engine and could lead to upside revisions to 2026 guidance.

Another analyst, Yiannis Zourmpanos, said Helios positions AMD as a turnkey AI platform provider, bundling MI455 GPUs, EPYC Venice CPUs, networking, and software.

System-level sales tie AMD’s top-line growth to client CapEx expansion, smoothing volatility between CPU and GPU demand cycles.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings, AMD carries a Strong Buy rating with a score of 4.74 out of 5, supported by an A grade for profitability and an A- for growth, although its valuation score remains weaker at D+.

Within the Wall Street analyst community, sentiment remains positive, with 41 analysts rating the stock a Buy or higher, 12 maintaining a Hold, and none assigning a Sell or lower.

In addition, Seeking Alpha’s analysts appeared bullish on the stock, assigning it a Buy rating.

data.title: Teledyne projects $6.37B revenue for 2026 while advancing unmanned and defense growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Teledyne Technologies (TDY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert Mehrabian, Executive Chairman, announced "the largest quarterly orders, sales and non-GAAP earnings and as well as operating margin in the company's history" in Q4 2025, and expressed optimism for 2026, citing both recent performance and new leadership, notably George Bobb as CEO and several senior executives with expanded roles.
  • Mehrabian reported, "Fourth quarter sales increased 7.3% from last year, while non-GAAP earnings increased 14.1%. For the full year, sales increased 7.9% and non-GAAP earnings increased 11.5%." He highlighted strong defense businesses, recovering short-cycle commercial businesses, and robust results from Teledyne FLIR and marine autonomous vehicles.
  • Mehrabian detailed capital deployment: "over $850 million spent on acquisitions throughout the year and a $400 million for stock repurchases within the fourth quarter," and added that free cash flow was about $1.1 billion for the second consecutive year, ending 2025 with a leverage ratio of 1.4x. He also announced the acquisition of DD-Scientific, a U.K.-based gas sensor manufacturer.
  • For 2026, Mehrabian stated, "we believe full year 2026 revenue will be approximately $6.37 billion and non-GAAP earnings at the midpoint will be approximately $23.65."
  • George Bobb, President and CEO, described a 3.4% increase in Digital Imaging sales, driven by Teledyne FLIR, with over 20% growth in infrared imaging components and subsystems. FLIR Maritime sales reached record levels due to demand from unmanned surface vessels. The company received its first production contract in the loitering munition market and was selected for U.S. Space Development Agency contracts, supplying infrared detectors to three of four primes. Non-GAAP operating margin for Digital Imaging increased 180 basis points to 24.7%.
  • Bobb noted Instrumentation sales rose 3.7%, with marine instruments up 3.3% and environmental instruments up 6.1%. Aerospace and Defense Electronics sales surged 40.4% from acquisitions and organic growth, though margins were pressured by newly acquired operations. Engineered Systems revenue fell 9.9% on delayed contracts, but segment operating margin rose 259 basis points.
  • Stephen Blackwood, CFO, reported, "cash flow from operating activities was $379 million compared with $332.4 million in 2024. Free cash flow...was $339.2 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, a record for Teledyne compared with $303.4 million in 2024." He stated net debt ended the quarter at $2.12 billion.

Outlook

  • Mehrabian provided 2026 guidance: "we believe full year 2026 revenue will be approximately $6.37 billion and non-GAAP earnings at the midpoint will be approximately $23.65." The company expects normal seasonality, with approximately 48% of sales and 46% of earnings in the first half of the year.
  • Blackwood gave Q1 2026 guidance of GAAP EPS between $4.45 and $4.59, with non-GAAP EPS between $5.40 and $5.50. Full year 2026 GAAP EPS is expected in the range of $19.76 to $20.22, with non-GAAP EPS in the range of $23.45 to $23.85.

Financial Results

  • Mehrabian said "fourth quarter sales increased 7.3% from last year, while non-GAAP earnings increased 14.1%. For the full year, sales increased 7.9% and non-GAAP earnings increased 11.5%."
  • Blackwood cited Q4 cash flow from operating activities at $379 million, free cash flow at $339.2 million, capital expenditures of $39.8 million, and depreciation and amortization expense of $84.6 million. Net debt at quarter-end was $2.12 billion.
  • Digital Imaging's non-GAAP operating margin hit 24.7%, Instrumentation's full-year margin reached 28.4%, and Aerospace and Defense Electronics saw a 40.4% jump in sales.

Q&A

  • Greg Konrad, Jefferies: Asked about organic vs. inorganic growth and outlook for short- vs. long-cycle businesses. Mehrabian responded that "most of the growth would be organic about 3.6%, nonorganic, a little over 4%, 4.2%" and he expects no contraction in short-cycle businesses for 2026.
  • Zachary Walljasper, UBS: Inquired about earnings cadence and implied deceleration. Mehrabian explained, "It's easier comps in Q1 versus last year...Traditionally, we've been about 48% in the first half of the year in revenue and 46% in profitability."
  • Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas: Questioned size of defense awards and tracking layer program contribution. Bobb stated, "that program for us will be north of $100 million over the next few years."
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham: Sought book-to-bill and unmanned business growth. Mehrabian shared Q4 book-to-bill ratios by segment, with total Q4 at 1.07 and full year at 1.08. He reported 2025 unmanned revenue at about $500 million, expecting it to grow to $550 million in 2026.
  • Jonathan Siegmann, Stifel: Asked about drivers of record underwater vehicle sales. Mehrabian noted full suite of manned and unmanned vehicles, with success in both U.S. and European markets.
  • Guy Hardwick, Barclays: Discussed M&A vs. stock repurchases. Mehrabian clarified "our primary driver has always been acquisitions," and that the company prefers tuck-in deals but is open to larger acquisitions "around $1 billion."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions focused on growth mix, margin sustainability, defense contract magnitude, and M&A strategy, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone, with interest in the underlying organic growth and recurring revenue.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, using phrases such as "we believe full year 2026 revenue will be approximately $6.37 billion" and "we remain confident in executing our strategy." When pressed, responses often provided specific breakdowns and acknowledged challenges, but overall emphasized stability and opportunity.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more upbeat, with expanded guidance and a greater emphasis on organic growth. Analyst sentiment shifted from cautious to more inquisitive, reflecting improved clarity on execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for both revenue and earnings was raised for 2026, with explicit targets for both figures, compared to more conservative commentary and uncertainty around government contracts in the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus remained on defense and unmanned systems, but management highlighted stronger performance in short-cycle businesses and a belief that none would contract in 2026, a change from last quarter's concerns.
  • M&A commentary was more optimistic about larger deals, whereas last quarter focused on caution around overpaying.
  • Book-to-bill ratios improved across several segments, and free cash flow and leverage metrics strengthened.
  • Management confidence increased, as reflected in both language and detailed segment outlooks, while analysts' questions were less focused on macro risks and more on execution and growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Mehrabian noted that Engineered Systems revenue was affected by delayed contract awards, but operating margin improved due to fixed-price contract performance.
  • Analysts asked about memory pricing and supply chain risks; Mehrabian stated, "we don't see a risk, net risk in that area."
  • No significant risks from commercial aviation exposure or FX volatility were flagged, with management describing both as minor or stable.
  • The company continues to monitor acquisition pricing and is selective about large M&A.

Final Takeaway

Teledyne Technologies concluded 2025 with record quarterly performance and enters 2026 with strong momentum, targeting $6.37 billion in revenue and over $23.6 in non-GAAP EPS. Management emphasized balanced growth across both long- and short-cycle businesses, robust free cash flow, and ongoing capital deployment through strategic acquisitions and select stock repurchases. The outlook for defense and unmanned systems remains particularly strong, and the company projects continued improvement in margins and cash generation, supported by both organic growth and strategic acquisitions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Anthropic revenue run rate surpasses $9B: report
data.description:
Anthropic's (ANTHRO) revenue run rate surpassed $9B at the end of 2025, notable as the maker of Claude is getting ready to raise additional funding, Bloomberg reported.

The company is in talks with investors such as Coatue Management, Singapore’s GIC and Iconiq Capital for its latest financing round, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter. Multiple sources added that the deal is oversubscribed, and Anthropic has already received commitments to raise at least $10B. The size of the round could top $20B, some of the people added.

The new funding could value Anthropic at around $350B, according to previous reporting. In contrast to Anthropic, OpenAI (OPENAI) is valued at about $500B, while xAI (X.AI) is valued at around $250B.

Anthropic did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Anthropic's annual run rate surpassed $5B in August 2025 and was nearing $7B as of October, as its enterprise and business customer base exploded. That same month, it was reported the company was eyeing a $9B revenue run rate by the end of 2025.

Competitor xAI recently announced a $20B Series E funding round that included investors such as Valor Equity Partners, Qatar Investment Authority, MGX, Nvidia (NVDA) and Cisco's (CSCO) venture capital arm, Cisco Investments.

Anthropic creates the Claude family of large language models and is a direct competitor with Microsoft-backed (MSFT) OpenAI (OPENAI), which produces the GPT line of AI models. OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar said recently that its annualized revenue run rate topped $20B.

data.title: Watts Water hits all-time high as KeyBanc raises on strong setup; M&A story builds momentum
data.description:
Watts Water Technologies (WTS) +4.3% to an all-time intraday high $304.42 in Wednesday's trading as KeyBanc upgraded the provider of residential and commercial flow control and protection products to Overweight from Sector Weight with a $340 price target, seeing the company's setup in 2026 as too compelling to ignore, particularly following the recently muted stock reaction to upward revisions.

While end-market visibility remains limited, KeyBanc's Jeffrey Hammond feels estimates are likely to move higher from current levels regardless of end-market improvement via sizable pricing tailwinds, outgrowth from data centers, and recent M&A, and he believes the building momentum within the attractive M&A story is underappreciated.

Watts Water (WTS) has quietly returned to executing more meaningful M&A, with ~$695M in deals since Q2 2023, and despite the flurry of recent deals, Hammond said the company has plenty of room to leverage its pristine balance sheet, and he senses the pipeline remains supportive of continued deal activity in 2026; the analyst also believes Watts Water has built a strong deal and integration process focused around bolting-on small and medium-sized assets.

"As such, we feel valuation ahead of the historical range is warranted given the combination of the clear path to earnings upside and organic/inorganic factors supporting earnings growth ahead of historical trends," Hammond wrote.

data.title: Sony will give up control of its TV business after spinning it off
data.description:
Sony Group (SONY) plans to spin off its TV and broader home entertainment hardware business into a global joint venture with China’s TCL, which will have majority control. Under a memorandum of understanding, TCL will own 51% of the new company and Sony 49%, giving TCL operational control. The joint venture will assume Sony’s (SONY) home entertainment hardware portfolio, including televisions and home audio equipment, and will operate globally once definitive agreements are signed and regulatory approvals obtained. The new company is expected to begin operations in April 2027.

The joint venture will retain the Sony and Bravia brand names on products. The move shifts Sony from being a standalone TV manufacturer to a minority partner focused on brand, picture processing, and higher-value entertainment segments. The JV will handle product development and design, manufacturing, sales, logistics, and customer service for Sony-branded and Bravia-branded TVs and audio gear. TCL is expected to supply its display technology and cost-efficient manufacturing and supply chain capabilities, while Sony contributes its picture and audio processing know-how and brand equity. 

"By combining both companies’ expertise, we aim to create new customer value in the home entertainment field, delivering even more captivating audio and visual experiences to customers worldwide," stated Sony (SONY) CEO Kimio Maki on the development.

data.title: Societe Generale said to cut 1,800 jobs by end of 2027 - report
data.description:
Societee Generale (SCGLF) (SCGLY) is planning 1.8K job cuts in France by the end of next year as part of a wider cost-cutting measure, according to a media report on Wednesday. That's out of a French workforce of about 40K.

Labor unions were informed this week about the layoffs, which are expected to be made via attrition instead of forced departures, the GCT union said, the Financial Times reported. 

Societe Generale (SCGLF), France's third-largest bank, did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Apple plans to integrate an AI chatbot into its operating systems: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) is reportedly working on an artificial intelligence chatbot that it plans to directly integrate into the operating systems of its iPhone, iPad and Mac, according to Bloomberg.

The chatbot, which is known internally under the code name Campos, will operate much like Siri, and it is expected to be released before the end of the year, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter. It will likely feature the conversational style of other successful chatbots, such as OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT or Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Gemini.

Apple shares had ticked up 1.6% during afternoon trading on Wednesday.

Earlier this month, Apple acknowledged it was tapping Google to help develop its next version of Siri.

"After careful evaluation, we determined that Google's technology provides the most capable foundation for Apple Foundation Models, and we're excited about the innovative new experiences it will unlock for our users," Apple said in a statement.

Similar to Google's Gemini or Microsoft's (MSFT) Copilot, the new version of Siri will likely be embedded in Apple's mainstay apps. It will also help users conduct web searches, summarize information, analyze files, and rely on personal data to help manage and complete tasks, the report said. However, since Apple has always placed importance on privacy and data protection, it's unknown how deep this last feature will go.

Apple might also choose to lean on Google's servers to host and run its new chatbot, the report said. Apple is already paying Google about $1B per year for access to its AI models. However, Apple is designing Campos in a way that would allow it to transition to other models in the future.

data.title: Tech Voices: Ripple CEO on crypto market, OpenAI on ads strategy
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse said he believes the crypto market will hit a record high in 2026.

“I’m very bullish, and yes, I’ll go on record as saying I think we’ll see an all-time high,” Garlinghouse told CNBC during an interview on Wednesday.

Ripple is the fintech behind XRP (XRP-USD) and RLUSD (RLUSD-USD).

  • OpenAI (OPENAI) CFO Sarah Friar defended her company's plans to insert ads in ChatGPT responses for certain U.S. users of its free service.

During a panel discussion at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Friar said OpenAI needed to establish "a strong business model" to bring artificial general intelligence, or AGI, to more people.

“Our mission is AGI for the benefit of humanity, not for the benefit of humanity who can pay,” Friar said at the event on Wednesday, according to Bloomberg.

Friar also addressed comments made the day before by the CEO of rival Google DeepMind that he was surprised OpenAI was introducing ads so early. 

“Early is a weird word,” said Friar. “In ad models, you have to be at scale. Sub-scale ad models don’t work, so that would be early. When you have 800 million weekly active users, you’re really far beyond many of the companies who started in that model.”

Google DeepMind is owned by Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL). OpenAI, which was co-founded by Tesla (TSLA) and xAI (X.AI) CEO Elon Musk, counts Microsoft (MSFT) as a major backer.

data.title: Dime Community targets sub-50% efficiency ratio and NIM expansion through 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dime Community Bancshares (DCOM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stuart Lubow highlighted ongoing execution on the company’s strategic plan, stating core EPS reached $0.79 for the fourth quarter, representing an 88% increase versus the prior year. He attributed this to “record total revenues of $124 million for the fourth quarter.” Lubow emphasized organic growth, noting, “Core deposits were up $1.2 billion on a year-over-year basis,” while underlining the absence of purchase accounting boosts from M&A. He also reported, “Business loans grew over $175 million on a linked-quarter basis and over $500 million on a year-over-year basis.”
  • Lubow outlined a strategic focus for 2026, including significant loan repricing opportunities: “As we have discussed previously, and as Avi will mention in his remarks, we have a significant amount of repricing assets in the next 2 years, which provides a tailwind for revenue growth.” He stated the aim to “operate at a sub-50% efficiency ratio” in 2026.
  • Thomas Geisel, Chief Commercial Officer, described expansion of commercial banking, noting, “Since I came on board in February, we have added the following capabilities. Fund finance… Lender finance… Mid-corporate… Sponsor Finance… Syndications… and lastly, geographic expansion.”
  • CFO Avinash Reddy reported, “Core EPS for the fourth quarter was $0.79 per share. This represents an 88% year-over-year increase.” He added, “Core pretax pre-provision net revenue of $61.5 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 represents approximately 163 basis points of average assets.”

Outlook

  • Reddy expects “modest NIM expansion in the first half of the year and more substantial NIM expansion in the back half of the year as the pace of the back book loan repricing picks up.” He detailed, “for the full year 2026, we have approximately $1.4 billion of adjustable and fixed rate loans… at a weighted average rate of 4% that either reprice or mature in that time frame.”
  • The company expects “core cash operating expenses, excluding intangible amortization for 2026 to be between $255 million and $257 million.” Reddy guided that “noninterest income” for full year 2026 is anticipated “to be between $45 million and $46 million.”
  • Provision for loan losses is expected “to be in the $10 million to $11 million area” for coming quarters, trending “down into the single digits” in the second half.

Financial Results

  • Core EPS was $0.79 for the quarter. Total revenues were reported at $124 million. Net interest margin (NIM) for the quarter was 3.11%, with a core figure of 3.09% excluding prepayment fees, up from 2.98% last quarter. Core cash operating expenses were $62.3 million, below guidance.
  • Total deposits increased by approximately $800 million versus the prior quarter. Cost of total deposits declined to 1.85%, down 24 basis points sequentially. Allowance to loans increased to 91 basis points.
  • Noninterest income was $11.5 million, above guidance.

Q&A

  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Asked about industries driving business loan growth. Geisel responded that “about $400 million of that came from the specialty groups that includes health care, lender finance, fund finance, sponsor and not-for-profit,” with health care showing the most momentum.
  • Fitzgibbon: Queried about New Jersey business. Reddy said, “somewhere between 8% to 10% of our portfolio is Northern New Jersey. A lot of clients that we followed over there.”
  • Fitzgibbon: Asked about SBA loan sale gains and catch-up in pipeline. Reddy replied, “Q4 was probably 2 quarters into 1, basically, is how I'd characterize it.”
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked about deposit growth and pricing. Reddy said, “to get a new customer in the door, you probably got to offer high 2s to low 3s on the money market, but it's probably coming with 20%, 30% DDA,” and added, “the actual cost of deposits or the spot rate on deposits at the end of the year was 1.68%.”
  • Moss: Inquired about rent-regulated multifamily loans. Reddy stated, “the pre-2019 book… is around $350 million at year-end 2025.”
  • David Konrad, KBW: Asked about non-interest-bearing deposits. Reddy replied, “seeing a floor of around 30% is probably reasonable, and we'd like to have that ratio eke up slowly over time.”
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Asked about cash deployment strategy. Reddy said, “I don't see that cash balance coming down significantly in the near term, absent some of the seasonality.”
  • Breese: Asked about floating rate loans. Reddy stated, “floating rate is probably somewhere between 35% and 40% of the balance sheet.”
  • Breese: Asked about prepayment activity. Reddy responded, “in the fourth quarter, it was probably 15%,” indicating a decline from prior quarters.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were inquisitive and focused on sustainability of growth, deposit pricing, specialty verticals, and loan book composition, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed on business momentum and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing strong results and execution. Lubow’s statements emphasized ongoing momentum and strategic clarity, while Reddy provided precise metrics and cautious guidance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained upbeat, with increased specificity on expansion plans and confidence in loan repricing tailwinds, while analysts’ tone was steady, probing for details but showing less skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language became more explicit, with detailed targets for efficiency ratio, NIM trajectory, and loan repricing for 2026–2027, versus a more general outlook in the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward industry vertical expansion and geographic growth, particularly in Manhattan and New Jersey, and highlighted the addition of new specialty lending teams.
  • Key metrics, including core EPS, NIM, and deposit growth, improved versus the third quarter, with management attributing gains to organic initiatives rather than one-time items.
  • Management’s confidence level increased, supported by more specific forecasts and operational milestones.
  • Analysts continued to focus on credit quality, loan growth by industry, and deposit trends, but showed increased interest in the sustainability of recent performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential runoff in municipal deposits and seasonality, which could impact deposit balances in the near term.
  • Credit risk in rent-regulated multifamily loans was addressed, with management emphasizing granularity and ongoing attrition but expressing comfort with the current portfolio.
  • The company is monitoring prepayment activity, which declined in the fourth quarter, and remains focused on managing CRE concentration ratio.
  • Analysts raised questions about deposit pricing, sustainability of loan growth in new verticals, and deployment of excess liquidity, all of which management addressed with specific strategies.

Final Takeaway

Dime Community Bancshares delivered strong organic growth in the fourth quarter, driven by record revenues, expanding specialty lending verticals, and disciplined cost and deposit management. Management’s detailed guidance for 2026 projects continued NIM expansion, a return to sub-50% efficiency ratio, and further diversification of the loan portfolio. With robust capital, rising non-interest-bearing deposits, and a clear focus on commercial banking growth, Dime aims to leverage repricing opportunities and operational momentum for continued earnings power in the coming year.
